CURRENT AFFAIRS.

. © . TIrish domestic politics have become interesting in
25 view of the probability of a new shuffle of the cards. A
~ practical reunion of our specialised wrangling politicians
—poor things!—has apparently reached the stage of

~ probability. Let us have a look at the forces at work.

Edward VIIL. really never had a chance, though
some of his followers felt—they are emotional rather
than thinking persons—at one time that he held the

ng cards. Screeching William is a fact, and facts
faced. ‘“Tiny Tim ’’ was, and is, a fact, and
William made the mistake seven years ago
agnosing him as a ‘ poisoned bullet;”’ He extracted
ind Screeching William is now covering him with

such emotional solicitude as torchlight processions are
born of! Tt is all going through life in Ireland, and
we will not dilate on the works of the engine of life as it
trundles along a’ the rate of ten yards an hour. Wil-
liam is a fact—an hysterical, well-meaning fact—whe
will, one day, be judged by God ; it isn’t for us to judge
him ; like all other men, he is a mystery which we can-
not fully comprehend. William is sure that he is right;
he is passionately sure that his opponents are utterly
wrong ; William has money; William can run a paper
practically regardless of ccst. William, we are sure,
would not offer a man sixpence to change his cpinions,
but there are men who know that William has money,
who do not know but that he may yet be on 4top5; Wil-
liam will always have a few champions who wcan prob-
ably persuade themselves that they, too, are pure-
souled heroes. Now if William of the Screeches were
merely a wealthy individual who preferred ‘to reign
anmongst the boys of the 'Pool rather than serve in Ire-
land, he might be expelled from the body national-in:a
natural way. But, unfortunately, however qualified
one’s opinion of William may be, it is impossible-to gk
enthusiastic on the other side, that has for its cl
journalist exponen’ the ‘‘ Freeman,”’ whose Mr. Sextor
is not on speaking terms with Mr. Redmond, and whose
Matty Bedkin is now a County Court Judge.

The Matty Bodkin affair has done the ‘‘ Freeman ** &
lot of damage, and the call cn the guarantors of the
late Exhibition will prebably create a feeling in some
fresh quarters against the ‘ Independent.”” The second
best thing that we can sec in this political world at the
moment is that the screeches of William should be
brought under partial control by becoming an ingre-
dient of a united Irish Party.

We are amongst the most disinterested persons:in
the country ; we have no personal animosity against any
man—our kindly personal relations with even Mr.
Edward Martyn and Mr. John Sweetman have never
been interrupted. We contemplate the wrangling poli-
ticians as a doctor contemplates a curable disease. Wil-
liam O’Brien is getting sympathy not so much on his
merits as in consequence:of the muddle, inefficiency
and jobbery amongst his opponents. As bad as the
‘“ Freeman ’’ is, we hold it from a national point of
view to be preferable to the ‘‘Independent.”’  The
¢‘ Independent ”’ that was responsible for the recent
Exhibition, as a result of which a bill of about £75,000,
it is anticipated by rumour, will be presented to the
unfortunate guarantors, is coquetting with William
O’Brien, even though it exerted whatever influence it
had to have the Councils Bill kicked out without an
effort to amend it.

What of John Redmond! He makes a splendid
Chairman when there is something orderly and strong
to preside over; he is not a great originating force. He
is the only thinkable Chairman of a working Irish
Party. Tt is easy to point out his limitations, but not
casy to answer the question—who could be substi‘uted
for him? William O’Brien has no whisper against Mr.
John Redmond’s chairmanship. And Mr. Sohn Red-
mond’s chairmanship, and the whole Pssty, are injured
and weakened so long as wealthy William is carrying
on a vigorous faction agitation; for the Party is not
strong and virtuous enough to be proof against faction.
Their ‘‘virile agitation” is rather theatrical, whilst
the virile faction of Mr. O’Brien on their flank is effec-
tively harassing. We would be sorry to trust the poli-
tical fortune of Ireland to the emotions of Mr. O’Brien,
and yet we would not be glad that the ‘ Freeman ™

part~
1had a?y ~ore rope_ than they have. 
The "Free-
man 
won t like, a unity arrangement, for such an 
a.rrangement would rather place the " Independent " 
on top of it--thougb, no doubt, the " Freeman " would, 
as nimbly as it could, attempt to adjust itself to the 
new situation. 
• 
For. our. part we trust, for the sake of the country, 
that Redmond, O'Brien, Dillon and Healy will strike 
u~ a working agreement. 
Such au agreement could not 
fail to leave the country politically in a more effective 
s:ate. 
For so long as William the Wealthy is in oppo-
s~tion, _there will be disquiet and undue apathy; Wil-
ham will become amenable to some discipline by being 
placed in harness again. 
It will be interesting to see 
what will happen when Tim goes back; it is quite pos-
si!>le t~at nothing very startling one way or the other 
will happen. The picture of Tim nobly effacing him-
s•elf for his country's good, as drawn by Mr. O'Brien, 
is not without its humorous side. 
Tim, who was a 
"poisoned bullet" irritating Mr. O'Brien's imagina-
tions about seven year,s ago, has undergone a transfor-
mation in that incomprehensible region-Mr. O'Brien's 
emotion; our own opinion is,· that Tim is pretty much 
the same now as then. "If peace," said Mr. O'Brien, 
u if peace there is to be, there is one man to whose 
broadmindedness and self-renunciation a very large por-
·tion of the credit will be due, and that is Mr. T. M. 
Healy" (c:P,eers). 
Vve suppose the "Poisoned Bullet" 
keeps a serious face on him whilst be is talking to Wil-
liam, but he must find it an ease when \.Villiam's back 
is turned, so tp.at he can become au "explosive bullet." 
What tragi-comedians a lot of our politicians are, to 
lie sure. 
Well, anyway, we hope they will strike up 
a wo"rking arrangement, and g~t to their business of 
politics as quickly as possible. 
We are tired of "virile 
agitation," " unity " m_eetings, Smithfield ructions, the 
.stick-in-the-mud comedy of North Leitrim, the farce 
of Edward VIII. 
\.Ve want to see some results, be they 
little or big, from all the political energy that is 
expended in this distracted country. 
A question of great _importance to those interested in 
Irish manufactured goods has arisen at the Millstreet 
(Co. Cork) local board. 
.When the half-yearly supplies 
.were being advertised for, there was a clause placed in 
the advertisement-by the way, we have not seen the 
advertisement, and, though we say it ourselves, there 
could be no better place for all these advertisements 
than the LEADEn-that no goods would be accepted that 
did not bear the Irish Trade Mark. 
When the goods 
arrived about on-e-fourth of them, · we understand, had 
not the Irish Trade Mark, and though the Guardians 
were convinced that the majority of the goods were 
Irish made, they rejected them in accordance with the 
terms of their advertisement as they did not bear £he 
Trade Mark. 
In so doing the Guardians, we think, 
acted in the only way consistently open to them in view 
of the terms of their advertisement. 
The clerk was 
directed to advertise again, and also to write to the 
firms who tendered pointing out why their goods were 
rejected. 
At a subsequent meeting ,the matter. came up 
again, and again the greater part of the goods had to 
be rejected in consequence of their lacking the Trade 
Mark. 
Once more the Guardians have advertised, and 
the matter will be dealt with shortly. 
' This Millstrcet matter .is very unsatisfactory. 
Afte1· 
all the voluntary effort that has been put into the work 
af obtaining and administering the Trade 1d:ark, one 
would think that the least the· manufacturers might do 
is apply for it. and use it. it does not· cost· mµch. 
Another point of view is that local bodies may be too 
drastic in insisting that all Irish goods accepted by them 
must bear the Mark; yet, in view of the long time that 
has ela,psed during which the Trade Mark might have 
been bbtained, we think the entire weight of the blame 
for such a state of affairs as has occurred at Millstreet 
must be placed 011 the manufacturers who have not 
gone to the trouble to obtain the Trade Mark. 
The' 
Trade Mark is largely clue to .the patriotic efforts of Mr. 
Boland, M.P., and to others. It is not a money making 
affair. 
Indeed, the Association that has to do with it 
has to depend 011 the voluntary aid of a section of the 
Press to advertise it for the good of the cause of Irish 
Industry. 
VVe ad,vertise t.he Trade Mark every week 
for nothing, as it was represented to us that the volun-
• 
Lary association that had to do with it should get volun-
lary aid from the LEADER and other papers, or else go 
without the help. 
In these circumstances it is extra-
Ol'C!inary that the Millstreet local board, .having, as a 
su.feguard, stipulated that Irish goods to be accepted 
by them should beai: the Mark, are put to all this extra 
trouble and expense by the apathy of some manufac-
turers. 
Many Irish· manufacturers happily are enter-
prising a.nd go-a-head; but many, on the other hand, 
are calculated to create a reaction against the Indus-
trial Revival. 
Some of them want too much; indeed, 
it is a wonder that they are not asking their workers to 
work for patriotism rather than for wages!

. "w. B. YEATS."

'vVe have received the following complaint about 
Limerick shops in relation to the stockiug of Irish goods.. 
In view of the meeting of the Industrial Conference in 
that city the complaint borders on the dramatic:-" I 
wish to draw attention through your va.luable paper to 
the great falling off in the supply of Irish manufactured 
goods to be got in Limerick. 
I am afraid that if this is 
allowed to continue the work of the Revival will in a 
short time have to be begun all over again, so far as this 
city -and its people are concerned. 
The greatest fa.ult 
lies with the drapery establishments and the stationers. 
In any of the former I found it impossible, only last 
week, to get navy-blue Irish-made serge. 
They bad. in 
many shops a small piece or ra.U10r remnant seemingly 
to be able to say that they kept Irish goods, and I was 
shown over a dozen different pieces of foreign make. 
Irish boots I liave long giveu up trying to get here. 
]n 
one large shop I was triumphantly informed that now 
' we have got Irish-made boots.' 
On inspection the 
Irish-made boots had reduced themselves to one pair 
ordered specia.Jly for me. 
In the stationers' shops the 
same depressing reply is given to inquiries for Irish post-
cards, 
newspaper wrappers, etc.--' \Ve don't keep 
Iri.sh ones.' 
I ha.ve come t.o the painful conclusion 
that, unless you want to be distinctly disliked by those 
who seJI, rlon't ask for Irish-ma.de goods. 
The reason 
of this is, I fear, that the number of those who ask for 
home-made articles are' so few that they can be ignored 
and have come to be looked on merely as cranks . . I must 
in fairness admit that there are a few praiseworthy· ex-
ceptions to this rule, but as far as the majority are con-

" Our readers remember, or at least we hope they re-
member, our account of the Munster Institute. The
Department of Agriculture are about to open at Cooks-
town, County Tyrone, a residential school for the train-
. ing of girls in dairying, poultry-keeping, and other
branches of work which usually fall to the lot of the
farmer’s wife and daughter. This institution is to be
kriown as the Ulster Dairy School. - The demesne of
Lcughry, about three miles from Cookstown Railway
Station, has been acquired. The house on the estate
= has accommodation for thirty resident pupils, and
there is a dairy and tillage farm of about 200 acres.
The students will be instructed in the use of modern
appliances, and in methods of working with the utensils
sssociated with small farmers’ houses. As it is an
Wlster School the courses will be made suitable for the
requirements of Ulster farm life. As at the Munster
Tnstitute, there” will be four sessions, each of about
eleven weeks’ duration. The fee for tuition, board and
lodging during one session is only three guineas. The
Ulster Dairy School opens on the-7th January. As
our readers know, there is quite a large number of in-
tending students always waiting their turn for entrance
into the Munster Institute. No doubt the number of
. applications for entrance to the Ulster Dairy School
will be greater than the places vacant, so that our
- readers would do well to write at once for application
forms to the Department, Merrion Street, Dublin. If
there is to be a rush on this school, we would like
zeaders of the LEADER to be well represented in the first
Batch of students.

CORRESPONDENCE.

SINN FEIN PRACTICE AND PREACHING.
London, 23rd Nov., 1907.

Dear Sir—A person named O’Connclly ‘‘ hastens to
a refutation >’ of my note of the 8th inst. It is unusual
to hasten this way; but, if possible, it is more unusual
to find a non-ignorant Sirah refuting a question. Fur-
ther, mere denial is not refutation. Will O’Connolly
inform the public whether it is true that (1) a member
stated at a meeting of the branch that it included a
volunteer; (2) this statement has not been refuted; 3)
a committee member of the branch admits that he was,
while denying that he is, a volunteer? I must advise
O’Connolly, however, not to hasten so fast as to lose
his temper by the wayside. IHaving hastened to a
refutation, R. O’Connolly is evidently anxious to dispel
the rumour. I feel that I must help -him. If the
volunteer has actually withdrawn from his regiment, he
must be in possession of certain documents. Let him
submit, these to anybody who desires to examine them.
This will clench the whole matter. Anything less is
idle and ineffective as the babbling brook. According
to O’Connolly the branch has been working bravely for
“over four years.”” As the organisation in Ireland was
not in existence so early, this must have been the germ
from which the whole organism sprung. That noble
genius and apostolic zeal should now be marred makes
the pity all the greater. Fraternity and tolerance are
two strong planks in the Sinn Fein propaganda. In my
note I simply asked for information on a certain point,
and R. O’Connolly, in reply, calls me ““ libel-monger.”’
Ye gods! what tolerance, fraternity, knowledge, cour-
tesy, and reasonableness are conjured up in that one
compound word, ‘‘libel-monger!”  Some people are
fond of concluding every act with a good motto. A
suitable one for R. O’Connolly when replying would be,
““for Sinn Fein’s sake hasten slowly.”’—Respectfully
yours,

W. B. HENNEsSY.

IRELAND AT WOREK.

BOTTLE MAKING IN DUBLIN.

7] " HE Ringsend side of Dublin is one of the industrial
parts of the City. On Friday night last, coming
on towards eight o’clock, it had more than the gloom
of high walls, chimney shafts and great stores, for it
was a murky, dismal night and damp under foot.:-T
was looking for Barrow Street on my way to pay-a
visit to the Dublin Bottle Works Co., manufacturers of
clear glass bottles. of all sizes. No doubt those con-
nected with bottle works would smile if they were told
that they are picturesque affairs in the night. Probably
they have long ceased to look at them with an eye for
scenic effect, and only think of them as hot places condu-
cive to a free flow of perspiration. In amongst the red-hof
bottles in the making as they move about over your
head at the end of iron tubes handled by the men; the
visitor is not exactly at home. No doubt he would get:
used to it if he went there often, but at the first visit
he could scarcely help an uneasy feeling that every
moment.a red-hot bottle is going to fall on his unworthy
head.- The fierce lights and the shadows as the menm
work along make up a picture that suggests a stage
scene where pikes-are being made for a revolution.
Being a cool-headed person, I remembered where I was
and who I was, and did not—as no doubt some other
editors might have done—did not for a moment imagine
that I was Robert Emmet or Wolfe Tone. Even as I
stood: talking in the sand store to the works manager,
Mr. Cassidy, by the flickering and uncertain light of ar
improvised lamp held up by one of the yard men, the
scene suggested conspiracies, strategies and insurrec-
tions. ¥ v
The first thing made in the bottle factory is gas. |
They make their own gas in producers from Scotch
ccal. The gas goes into a great furnace, and the gas and
air in the furnace make up between them a pretty
temperature of upwards of 4,000 degrees (Four
thousand) Fahrenheit Into this furnace the glass ingre-
dients—chiefly composed of sand—go for a period of
about 3% hours; and at the end of that time yom ‘
get a molten mass of stuff ready for the bottle makers.
So brilliant is the flame in the furnace that Mr. Cassidy
gave me a blue glass to place before my eyes as one of
the workmen lifted the door of the furnace. AsT have
said, sand is the chief ingredient in glass making, “indeed
it is the only one of the ingredients that contains glass.
Unfortunately the sand has to be imported; it-comes
from Rouen, France. It is said that there is suitable sand
at the top of some mountain, or mountains, “in ‘grey
Donegal, but that the cost of placing 1‘:, on the-market
is such that for practical purposes it might as well not
be there at all; there is also talk of sand at or near

Portadown.  Mr. Cassidy told me that they would
be only too glad to get the sand in Treland if they could,
so if this should reach the eyes of anyone who thinks
he has suitable sand for this purpose he had better send
on a sample to the bottle works at once.

I understand that it takes a period of six years to be-
come a fully fledged clear-glass bottle maker. The bottle
works are going night and day; one batch of workers
eommence at six in the morning and leave off at 5-15 in
the evening; another batch come on at six in the even-

. ing and leave off at 5-15 in the morning. There are

five hands to what is called a liole—th> hole is a hole in
the furnace through which ths molten glass is taken
out on the end of an iron tub.. The lump of molten
stuff at the end of the tube goes through several hands,
it is roughly shaped, it is placed in a mould and blown by
& workman whose cheeks are guaranteed not to crack
under more than ordinary pressure; the neck is cut off
and a special neck is welded on. The moulds are an
expensive item of a bottle works—by the way, the
moulds are made in Dublin. Bach customer probably
wants his name and trade mark or other particulars
impressed on his bottles, so that the number of moulds
in work are considerable, and a mould is an expensive
thing. The bottles are stacked in kilns at a fairly high
temperature for twenty-four hours, after which the kiln
is allowed to gradually cool and the bottles are cleared
out of the kiln at the end of three days and brought to
the extensive warehouse where they are sorted and
packed.  The bottle-blowers must have fine athletic
cheeks, and they would probably regard the blowing of
the biggest wind instrument in the loudest of brass
bands only as child’s play. I blew one bottle, and I
can assure the reader one was quite enough for a night
for my untrained blowing machinery. Liquid glass, if
I may use the term, can be pulled out in a fine thread
or blown apparently as thin as a soap bubble.

The output of the work at present is about two hun-
dred gross a week; but after Christmas it is expected
that there will be six holes working; there are four
working at present—two by day and two by night.
We see by the Department’s Returns of Imports and Ex-
ports that bottles to the value of £83,586 were imported
into Ireland in the year 1904. Of course, these include
black and coloured bottles, and the Dublin Bottle Works
Co. only make clear glass bottles. The field for this
Dish industry in the home marke$ alone is expansive,
and if England sends us bottles to-day there is no
reason why, if the home market support the Irish in-
dustry on its merits, that we should not send bottles
to England to-morrow.

The works at Barrow Street were only set going in
May last, and as there are at present forty-one hands
employed, and  as the output is about two hundred
gross a week, we may take it that the support given
the new industry by the Irish traders has been fairly
considerable, though, in comparison with the possible
trade, it is as yet only small. They have customers in
various parts of the country. In Dublin such firms as
Hovenden and Orr, Thwaite’s, Twinem’s, O’Brien, of
Henry Place; Woods, Webb and Co., Cantwell and
M‘Donald, Power’s Distillery, Kinahan and Co.; in
Cork, John Daly, Ogilvie and Moore; in Waterford,
Michael Sullivan and Garvey’s; in Fermoy, David Sul-
livan, and so on. I only mention a few by way of
sample, as catalogues of names are not very interesting
reading. The works are supported in Limerick, Wex-
ford, Carlow, Enniscorthy, Kilkenny, Gorey. The
Department of Agriculture and Technical Instruction
are also on their books as customers. Some of the
accounts are pretty heavy too; one firm—Messrs.
Twinem’s, of Dublin—took over one hundred gross
within a fortnight recently. Ball-stopper mineral water
bottles are made here in large quantities; indeed, the

Dublin Bottle Works Co. were the first to make this
class of bottle in Dublin. Hovenden and Orr use a
large amount of this class of bottle for their mineral
waters, and I was interested to hear that they have
patented a new bottle, or rather a mew stopper, from
which great things are expected. S11901a1 glass bottles
are made here for Miss Purser’s stained glass factory.
The glass is' made as bottles, as some special effect is
got that way, T understand ; and the bottles are cut up
at the stained glass factory. LA %

A large number of hottles are still imported into this
unhappy dumping-ground for all the world. There is
also an import trade done in second-hand bottles from
England. I am not a microbe expert, but bottles col-
lected from who knows where, perhaps some of them
from houses where people lived suffering from diseases
of various and virulent kinds, dumped over here don’t
suggest sweetness unalloyed. I don’t know anything of
the power of bottles to carry disease, but I understand
that in cases where the glass bubbles that one observes
frequently on the glass of bottles are broken, they offer
facilities to microbes. TFowever, as I am not a microbe
specialist, I had better not go into these matters, but
aryway the importation of second-hand bottles goes on.

The premises of the Dublin Bottle Works Co. were
previously occupied by a timber yard, and the whole
plant, buildings and chimney stack had to be erected
fresh on the ground, and I understand all the newest
appliances were put in, including the continuous gas-
generating furnace. There is plenty of room for ex-
pansion, and the obtaining of more labour offers no
difficulties, so that all that is wanted is that the Irish
traders should buy Irish bottles in preference to im-
ported bottles. Mr. Cassidy, the Works Manager, has
had experience at the other side as well as in Ireland,
and he speaks highly of the men under him. The men
are most anxious to push this industry in the country,
and are, we are assured, doing their utmost to give
general satisfaction to their employers and to the

traders. Mr. Cassidy says there ought to
be five hundred hands employed at the
industry in Dublin. The Dublin Bottle Works

Co. don’t ask or expect any favour from Irish traders.
They ask a trial and a preference, other things being
equal ; and we hope that all our readers who have clear_
bottle contracts to give out, and who have been in the
habit of importing their supplies, will at least give this
new Irish-producing firm a trial. D. P. M.

ENNISKILLEN.

\/"\/’ TAT is true of Enniskillen—where the dragoons
/ come from—is true of many an Irish town at
present. In the days when agriculture was flourishing,
it was a thriving place; splendid markets and fairs,
good business—a living for everybody. Agriculture
has, however, declined, and does not require so many
centres of distribution as formerly; the surrounding
population has declined, and does not call for so many
necessities and luxuries, and the markets have declined
—especially the butter markets; owing to the general
adoption of the creamery system, farmers do not now
find it necessary to come so often into town to sell
their supplies, and they buy their groceries and things
at one of the hundreds of country shops which have
sprung up like mushrooms all over the country. The
consequence is, that the ‘‘thrade’’ is diverted from
the town, which is now pretty well reduced to the con-
diton of the Chinese quarter referred to by an Ameri-
can humourist—where the inhabitants lived by doing
one another’s washing. The grocer preys on the
draper, the draper on the grocer, and the publican
mops up anything that is left. That is inland town
life in Ireland in one sentence. Not that we have not

something in the shape of industries in Enniskillen.
We have thelocal browery and mineral water manufac-
tory—which finds its markets in any town but its own.
Places so far afield as Belfast can do with the Ennis-
killen article, but the local taste is fastidious, and

ther industry. is an extensive concern owned by the
Scottish Co-operative Society, where bacon is cured,
and, butter is made, and othet items of agricultural pro-

- twenty years ago, and has been progressing at such a

~ rate that at present it turns over somethinglike :£250,000

. fitted it with the most modern and up-to-date

mnc:hmeryz_ electric light, etc., and ave continually
adding to ib. Whilst the town traders were concontrat-
ing their energies on municipal politics the industrious
Scot was working up this .fine business. Derry,
Aughrim, and the Boyne, the sunburst, and the ‘“no
f@r distant date,” have all been receiving a lot of atten-
‘tion.  Latterly the ‘‘tide of emigration’—a phrase
which may now be classed almost with the ““ no far dis-
tant ’—has had a generous shave of discussion. Mean-
while our canny friends have been confining themselves
o those prosaic articles—butter, and bacon, and eggs—
and have once more demonstrated the difference
betwen talk and work. Derry, Aughrim, and the
Boyne still remain but green memories; the ‘“no far
distant ’ does not appear to have come much nearer,
but the butter and bacon and eggs have developed into
a fine property, with an enormous turnover, providing
considerable employment to the townspeople, and,
‘doubtless, a handsome profit to the proprietors. And
the employment is not one-sided either—a mixed staff,
‘practically all townspeople, about equally divided
between the two sections of politics, who seem to have
no trouble in working harmoniously. In this matter,
the much-abused Scotchman can provide a headline
for many an institution in the county and province.

Some local people have begun to rub their eyes
Jatterly, and to see what has been going on under their
very noses. The only queer thing is that it has taken
twenty years for them to see it. A feeling appears to
be gaining ground that the foreigner should not have
all this good thing to himself, and it is stated that the
said foreigner sometimes takes advantage of the lack of
-competition to lower the price of pork, etc., to an
unjust figure. Whether this is so, I cannot say. But
I presume human nature in Scotland is pretty much the
-same as everywhere else, and most people going into a
market buy as cheap as they possibly can. At any
Tate, a new locally-owned bacon factory is now called
for, and some steps are being taken to start it. There
is plenty of room for it, and everyone—probably includ-
ing the Scotchmen—will be glad to see it succeed. The
latter have evidently no intention of resigning business
if it does. They are about to put up a piggery which
will accommodate 1,000 pigs. They will buy “slips,”
-and feed them on to the proper killing weight—the
most profitable period of feeding, it appears—and so
will have a triple advantage—the profit on the feeding,
the option of lying out of a dear market, and the con-
trol of their quantities, so as to keep their staff and
machinery uniformly employed.

Starting a full-grown factory is no child’s play.
‘Where the capital has been subscribed, the buildings
and machinery erected, the manager—on whom almost
-everything depends—secured, and the staff got into
‘working order, then the market has to be provided,
‘and the ordinary hum-drum work of the place only
‘begins.  The Scottish Co-operative Society have the
-enormous advantage of having their market within
-themselves. A local company will have to hunt up a
‘market. Nevertheless, many of the local people are
:sanguine of success, and there are hopes of aid from the
Department. It is one of the tragedies of this country
‘that we are always depending on help from somewhere
‘or another. 7
~ Last year a determined effort was made to establish
‘a linen factory. Unfortunately it did not succeed. It
‘is hard to coax money out of your average successful
‘merchant to start manufactures. He does not see his

way clearly to the profits. His mind runs in a narrow
groove. He buys articles at one price, and sells them
at another. Perhaps he fakes them, and increases his
profit.  Perhaps he palms off shoddy as Irish manufac-
ture. Perhaps he does a little at, the gombeen busi-
ness. In any case, where he lets money out he likes to
make sure of it coming back again, with more along
with it. e prefers mortgages to manufactures; they
are genorally more suited to his order of ability. Manu-
factures are strange animals to him; he does not under-
stand them; he will not have full control. ~And who
knows but that these gentlemen who are working so
hard to get the capital subscribed, may not be grinding
some axe of their own? So he -thinks he will wait
awhile, and see how it gets on before investing. And
there you are. '

Enniskillen has one queer asset—the county gaol.
Like Mullingar—a big, dirty, forbidding-looking place,
that frowns at you when you step out of the station,
and takes away your appetite, and damps your enthu-
siasm for the pretty park on the hill above it. It is
a bugbear, an incubus, a white elephant. The County
Council are in a perpetual worry as to what on earth
they will do with it. The latest solution appears to be
to break it up into road metal. That seems a pity, as
the dressed stone it contains must be of great value.
Why not advertise it? Offer it, lock, stock and barrel,
at a nominal rent, on lease, to any company who will
ccme and transform it into a factory. It is well
situated, in a fine railway centre, close to the station.
It has a fine site, a vast supply of building material,
and a town beside it yearning for factories. It is quite
pessible some firm would be glad to jump at the offer,
and, in any case, it would be no harm to try.

Let us hope this new bacon factory will come to
something. If it doesn’t, those thousand porkers on
Coleshill will be grunting derision at us for evermore.

From a social point of view, perhaps it would be as
well not to lift the curtain too high. On consideration,
however, just a peep may do some good—the first step
towards improvement is to realise the necessity for it,
and, after all, I don’t know that we are socially much
worse or much better than most other towns.

We are divided, religiously and politically, about
equally. The ‘“ Idolaters” have a majority in num-
bers, but the other side have it in votes, having the
advantage of including most of the landlords and pro-
perty owners on their side. The result is a constant
struggle for supremacy between the two camps, heat at
election times, indignation when a job comes to be
filled, for, true to their traditions, our friends of the
Garrison have carefully abstained from appointing a
single Catholic to any position they control—with the
exception of street scavenger. The way in which these
jobs are wire-pulled was exemplified a couple of years
ago, when a nurse at £15 a year was to be appointed
for a local dispensary.  The circular issued by our
enterprising friends got into one of the ‘‘ Idolater’s’’
hauds, and was published in the LrapEr at the time.
It is seldom such a bungle is made—they have reduced
wire-pulling and jobbery to a fine art, these Garrison
people, and not often is their hand exposed. Subtly,

Which is another way of saying that the Playwright
brings up before you and around you, short though the
piece is, what you feel must have been the atmosphere
of Irish society in the process of conversion. But,
besides that triumph of effect, there is in the dialogue
and working out of the plot a manly strength and
sweep and a literary uLap that marks, I should say,
the dramatist. T don’t feel, however, that there is any
need to congratulate Mr. Purcell. To me he seems to
be one of those writers who know what they can do,
and will do it. g

As to, the acting, for reasons of their own, the Ulster
Players choose not to disclose the names of their actors.
All that T will say, therefore, is that the acting was, on
the whole, worthy of the piece. I have never scen a
Pagan Chieftain half as Pagan or half as much a Chief-
tain as this six-foot Gorman MacRory. And his fol-
lower, Cellach, was a fitting henchman for such a
master.

‘“The Enthusiast ’’ is a topical modern piece. The
young man comes for a holiday from his studies in
Belfast to his County Antrim home full of ideas for
the improvement, economic and other, of his father’s

neighbours. He proposes to hold a meeting of ¢ all
creeds and classes’’ to start a general co-operative
movement. The old man can’t very well understand

it all, and falls asleep during Jimmy’s eloquent explana-
tion of the scheme. Jimmy, however, prevails and the
meeting is held. . It breaks up in a row. Jimmy him-
self is saved with difficulty. He comes home (I should
have said that the old woman at home in the kitchen
keeps the audience in good humour while the meeting is
in progress below in the meadow), to be soundly rated
by the old man for having disgraced him—*‘an Elder
of the Church.” At length, however, the delinquent is
forgiven and peace is restored. Tea is ready in the
room above the kitchen. The other members of the
household retire to drink the tea, but Jimmy has no
stomach for it. e remains in the kitchen disconso-
late. ‘“ Might he possibly yet succeed?’ he solilo-
quises. ‘“ Tum, tum, rat-at-a-tum!”’ The drums are
out! and the curtain drops on the enthusiast’s sigh of
despair.

Such is the slight framework of the piece, but in
that framework is held the making of half-an-hour’s’
irresistible fun and laughter.  Jimmy’s brother Sam
coming home from market with the ‘“Police Budget

for the men, and the *‘Christian Herald ’’
for the women of the family, Rab, the
servant  boy’s delight in the Divorce News
of the former; Aunt Marget’s horror at the

thought of bringing Catholics and such like into their
meadow, and the every word and situation of the piece
are indescribable. ~Of course in such a piece the acting
is everything, and on this occasion it is no exaggeration
to say that the acting was everything. Every one of
those concerned must surely, I should say; have lived
through such a piece in real life, if not all at once, at
least in bits.

Which reminds me. Belfast, big as it is, is dis-
tinctly provincial and villagy, contrasted with Dublin.
I can’t imagine the Dublin Irish Literary Theatre
appealing so intimately to an audience as their brothers
here in Belfast manage to appeal to us. And the
explanation is not far to seek. I should fancy that in
those Revival movements, literary and other, you must
be local before you can hope to be national.

A travelling Company of trained Players to tour the
country with a stock of Plays has been suggested. It
would be a good part of a good idea, but a better part
of the idea would be to have as many local Plays and
Players as possible to keep the localities alive between

and its House of Lords, apply to this class of office,

The law, as I have mentioned, does not care whether
there is security or no for these contracts, and each
onc of the latter provides that in the event of the pre-
mium not being paid when it falls due (often weekly),
or within a certain time thereafter, the company—even,
I presume, during the last foew weeks of the currency of
the contract—can lapse the policy, and forfeit every
penny that the unfortunate policy-holder (often a poor
person) has paid in premiums. Certainly a clause is
often inserted in such policies stating that loans and
surrender values are available after the policy shall
have been five years in force, but as no amount is defi-
nitely stated, I unhesitatingly assert that this clause is
of little or no account. It is obviously left in the com-
pany’s power to give a very small fraction of the money
paid in, and say—*¢ Here is your surrender value accord-
ing to our actuary’s calculations.”’” And where is the
pclicy-holder’s redress? Echo answers—‘‘ Where?”’
The business is done, as a rule, amongst wage-earners—
the very people whom the law shoudl be particular to
protect; but is it not deplorable that their hard-earned
cash may be thus closed upon, and by companies that,
in return for the premiums they receive, take absolutely
no risk on the life of the person named in the policy,
or give practically no interest for the use of the money?

If we had in Ireland an Irish Mutual Insurance Com-
pany working on the lines of the Mutual Offices of every
progressive country, managed and controlled by Irish-
men, and for the Irish people, the latter would have an
incentive to habits of forethought and thrift, that would
enable them to provide against penury and the work-
house in their old age. But, how is such a company to
be inaugurated? It could be done in a few months if
people would only individually write on a sheet of paper
how much insurance (1) Fire, and (2) Life, they would
be ready to effect in it, giving their full names and
addresses. This was done in March last year, and
many thousands of pounds were promised through the
Leaper. But £20,000 is needed for the Court of Chan-
cery as security, before a policy, worthy of the name,
can be written. The question is—are there 2,500 Irish
men and women in Ireland prepared each to purchase
£10 worth of insurance in such a company —5,000 per-
sons buying £5 worth each would, of course, also meet
the case. What we want is £20,000 for investment as
security through the Court of Chancery, and about
£5,000 to go on with—securing new business, paying
claims, etc.

Smaller or larger premiums than £5 or £10 would,
of course, be accepted, and if promised to be made pay-
able yearly, half-yearly, or quarterly, or monthly, fort-
nightly or weekly, instead of a single payment, so much
the better. ;

I merely give an idea as to how the money might be
raised. No shareholders are wanted—or desirable—as
it is not intended to barter away the company, when a
success, to some English or other foreign concern, as has
been done previously more than once in Ireland.  The
Mutual Company, I propose, would be owned solely by
the policy-holders—.e., those people who insure in it;
and they would receive the whole of the profits without
deductions such as dividends to shareholders. Once
an Insurance Company has been started shareholders
are of little use except to appropriate to themselves a
large share of the profits, which, without them, would
go to the policy-holders. Let us all, therefore, endeavour
to establish a Mutual Insurance Office—none has ever
yet been established in Ireland, by the way—and let us
put our money in it. No institution could be safer.
The enterprise is one in which every man, woman, boy
and girl in Ireland can lend a hand in building up.

THE UNICORN FROM THE STARS,

T HE most striking quality of the latest Abbey pro-

duction is an elusiveness of form which defies any
definition of drama, ancient or modern, classic or
Tomantic, realist, symbolist or new Hellenic. It has
not the ordered action of the Greek, nor the vivid inci-

. dent of the Elizabethan, nor even the emotional rhythm

characteristic ' of Maeterlinckian drama.  Martin
Hearne, a dreamer of dreams, thinks he is elected to
destroy the artificial order of the world, to bring men
back to the “old splendour and the wildness of the
clean, green earth;’’ in-a word, to restore that primi-
tive state so dear to the imaginings of the latter-day
disciple of Nietzsche’s gospel of the Super-man or
Super-brute. Restraining forces are Martin’s uncle,
Thomas Hearne, who represents practical work, and
Father John, who, like Martin, has thirsted for the
supersensuous vineyard, and aspired to share in the
might of the unicorn, but feels that such dreams are
only for the ‘“old holy times.” A lawless rout of
tinkers gives Martin his cue. He first fires their
imaginations with drink, to teach them the ‘‘ exultation
of enthusiasm and the frenzy of contemplation, and all
life that is a flame of fire! 7 Then, under the banner
of the Unicorn of Power, he sets out to destroy material
things, and work, and the law that was ‘‘ the first bite
of the apple,”” and all that stands between us and God.
He begins by setting fire to his uncle’s workshop, and
at this the second act closes upon a scene of grotesque
and lurid force, the one gripping scene of the Play.

" Around Martin, as he kindles the straw, dances a wild,

bright carnival of beggar-folk, the picturesque rascal-
dom of highway and moor, screaming and waving skinny
arms aloft. It is Holbein himself, this dance of death,
its grim diablerie all the more striking in contrast to the
hero’s character.

‘The third act finds Martin in trance on a hill-side.
The police are on his track and that of his followers who
‘take him to be their leader and saviour; for the tramps
and tinkers- are rather unconvincingly represented as
having serious political and patriotic aims. There is a
long, wearisome discussion as to whether Martin will
wake from his journey on the other side. At last he
wakes, his ears heavy with the ‘‘music like a great
clashing of swords.”” His followers call on him to
carry their vague enterprise through. But his sword,
like so many weapons most cunningly wrought, is a
sword only of the spirit, his battles fought only in the
soul. He puts his followers off with phrases high-
sounding_but rapid. ‘‘My mission is revelation, not
reformation.”” He learnt in ‘‘ one moment, that
fiery moment when only one thing matters, when we

- are part of the Host of Heaven,”” that his war should be

not against matter in the world outside, but against the
enemies that lurk within the spirit. “ We must put
out the senses! We must put out the sun and the stars
as I put out this candle and this.”” And so the foolish
business goes on, and, with resonant unmeaning words,
he repudiates responsibility for the passionate hopes he
has awakened.  Yet the author’s intention appears to
be to make him admirable, and when a chance shot in a
scuffle kills him, and he dies whigpering—*‘ The vine-
yard, the vineyard!”” the tramps and uncles uncover in
reverence and sorrow. .

Tt may be seen that this piece, written by Mr. Yeats
in collaboration with Lady Gregory, is full of sound and

fury; but as far as the present writer can discern, it

leads where there is nothing. Its theme is the common-
place of the tragic poe(?—‘the paradox between a man’s
aspirations and his abilities, between will and efficiency -
—the paradox of Job, of Oedipus, of Hamlet, and of
Brand' (from whom Mr. Yeats has consciously or un-
consciously absorbed a good deal). But Mr. Yeats does
not handle this theme dramatically. No definite issue
is raised between the warring elements of will "and
circumstance, and the conflict is dissipated in a luxuriant
waste of dialogue mainly composed of highly-coloured
but fatiguing pseudo-philosophisms, such'as "' Where all
work ends life begins,”” and ‘‘ Destruction in the life-
giver.” The colour is the one thing that rescues this
Play from complete insignificance. ~The compelling -
music of the Yeats phrase and the spark!e of the Gregory
satire distract one’s attention from the ineptitude of the
matter. I have already referred to the scene at the end
of the second act. It is the only interesting moment in
the Play, but, when spoken by F. J. Fay, the Yeats
phrase always awakes response, however that response
may be cooled when we find that the “little infinite
thing ’ in search of which the hero wanders about the
stage, 'is a tallow dip which he waves wildly about with
reckless disregard for grease-spots. - This candle business
convicts the authors of lack of stage-sense.

We have not had much opportunity, in this country,
of studying art (with a big or a little ‘“ A ’’). Hence
the present piece seems stranger to us than if we were
familiar with the names of Maeterlinck, Verhaeren and
Conperus in the Netherlands, von Hofmannsthal and
Volmveller in Germany. We might be better fitted to
appreciate Mr. Yeats’ work if we knew more of a cer-
tain European literary movement which has now almost
spent its force. The masterpiece of this movement is
perhaps Maeterlinck’s one-act Play, ‘‘ Les Aveugles,”
and its last exhausted expression the same author’s
‘“ Aglavaine et Sélysette.”” The quality of this style of
drama is its immediate and intense appeal at once to
intellect and to emotion and to a deever sense than
either of these, by means of mood pictures such as the
image of expected horrer in “ Les Aveugles ’’; a minor
characteristic is obscurity of language. ‘‘ The unicorn
from the Stars ’’ seems to me to be a product of the ebb-
tide of this movement, resembling its predecessors in
obscurity and faltering style, but not in charm and
power. The most richly sensuous of the later symbolists,
the Viennese, Karl Gustav Volméller, has much in com-
mon with Mr. Yeats. But, unlike Mr. Yeats, Vol-
moeller, in his three-act piece ‘“ Catherina von Armag- -
nac,”” has built up a sound dramatic plot from which
his verse evolves in crashing chords and glowing symbols.
Mr. Yeats’ great lack is form.- He allures his imagin‘n-
tion to riot unbridled. The result in this case is that
the only appeal of his piece rises from the personality of
the actor. The natural reflection is that the Abbey
Theatre actors deserve some better scope for their talent.
Mr. Fay’s company is.unique, and should not be lost
upon a disgusted and uninterested Dublin, which has at
least sufficient discernment to reject as intellectual
pabulum the backwash of Continental ebb-tides.

we do not appear to have, .ourselves, originated a
generally approved variety. This is not as it should be
in a potato-growing country, and it is satisfactory ‘to
note that our leading agriculturists are now waking up
to the importance and necessity of the case. The De-
partment has carried out experiments of a most interest-
ing and valuable nature in respect of Irish potatoes
within the past few years. There have been trials
between Irish, English and Scottish seed, and the Irish
potatoes came out best. These results open up a good
trade for Trish seed potatoes if the farmers are equal
to same.  Hitherto, we have been importing seed
potatoes from Scotland ; now, it is found, as a result of
the above trials, that Irish seed is the best and most
reliable, and the English farmers are willing to take
seed from us if we are able to supply them with it in
quantity and true to kind.

In reference to these trials, the ““ Quarterly Journal *’
of the Department, published last January, in the
course of an article on the subject, says:—‘ The average
yields show that with each of the three varieties, Irish
secd has given heavier crops in England than Scottish
seed. This is a striking result, and it is of the highest
inportance to those interested in the seed potato trade
in TIreland, since it shows that Irish seed has given
heavier crops in England than Scottish seed.”” The
““Journal * gives a number of details essentially neces-
sary to the trade, and it sums them up as follows :—*‘(1)
The cultivation of varieties that are popular in England ;
(2) the necessity for growing and supplying only seed
that is pure and true to name; (3) careful selection of
the tubers before despatch ; (4) early lifting of the crop;
(5) an endeavour to meet requirements in general and
to supply what the purchaser is prepared to pay for,
even though it necessitates a departure from the regular
practice adopted at home.”  Now that scientific
methods have entered into potato culture—sprouting the
seed before it is planted, and spraying of the haulm as
preventative of the blight—there is no reason why a
larger area of potatoes should not be grown in Ireland
by the larger class of farmers to meet the English de-
mand for seed. With a fair field—say, 10 tons to the
Trish acre—and a good price, the crop should give ex-
cellent returns. If there are obstacles in respect of
labour and transit facilities, they should not prove in-
surmountable.  Another great beunefit which might
accrue from the development of this trade in seed pota-
toes would be the utilization of our cut-away peat bogs.
The latter are excellent soil' in which to freshen up and
renew seed potatoes. ~ When potatoes grown on clay
soil are transferred to bog, or moor, they become fresh
and vigorous, and make far better seed for clay than
potatoes grown in itself. In fact it should be the rule
to grow none but bog-seed in clay soil, and none but
clay-seed in bog. -

Another experiment now being carried out by the
Department is the growing of early potatces for the
Cross-Channel markets, and, as far as it has gone, there
is claimed for it very successful results. The present
season was too wet and cold to allow of a good growth
of the tubers, and the crop did not mature as well and
yield as good return as the two previous years, but
prices were better, so that the growers are said to have
had a fairly agreeable experience. Demand for Irish
early potatoes seems to have caught on to the Cross-
Channel merchants, and the present year the principal
raising centres were visited by a party of Scotch buyers,
and several lots were purchased as early as May at a
remunerative price per acre. The prices reported by
the Department run from £25 to £38, and from £30 to
£40 per acre, the purchaser lifting the potatoes himself
and paying transit expenses.

The following statement, inter alia, from the Depart-
ment’s report gives an idea of the trade:—‘ One leading
grower in the Rush district this year sold his cropof 10
acres in the field to a Scotch merchant at the rate of
£30 per acre, and in return the latter largely employed
girl workers from Achill Island .when the tubers were
being raised and despatched to markei.”” The Rush
district in Co. Dublin has a time-honoured reputa-
tion for successful early potato growing; but now that

it ought to be borne in mind that there are thousands
of people who are glad of any excuse for not going to
any trouble in the cause. There are large numbers
of people only too glad of the excuse to be able to say
that the League is narrow.  Well, of course, a big
movement is. a complex thing that cannot be run by
clock-work, but in the matter of laying themselves open
to being called narrow, many may have given. unneces-
sary points to those who were anxious for excuses to
keep doing nothing.  There are any amount of people
in the English-speaking districts who know that they
ought to be in the Irish-Ireland movement, and who
could be gently and easily shoved there if they were not
given excuses for staying out. Of course, T admit that
many people have a great ingenuity for finding excuses
for doing nothing, and if the movement were conducted
by saints they would find something to cavil at,

The time has come for a few years of broad toler-
ance in the language movement. We have made our
fierce assaults and frontal attacks, and we have done
well. Personally, I think the ground conquered and
held has been very considerable, and only for the heavy
fighting there would be very little movement now. The
point of view of the country has been materially altered,
if not yet shifted, to its proper place; in the matter of
printed books and Trish writers of various degrees of
ability, we are in a position to-cay that few shrewd
Gaelic Leaguers ten years ago would dare to have
hoped. A few years of development, and give and
take, after the hard blows and the fierce attacks, are
wanted, I think, to strengthen and secure the Irish-
Treland position in the public opinion of the country,
and then probably—if things are not to our liking—
the time may be opportune, and we can all go out
again, and make direct attacks on every man and every
thing that blocks the way. D. P. M.
